summary: Germany occupied France when I was a little girl during World War II. My father had to join the French Army and was later taken as a German prisoner of war. It was a difficult time for my family under German control.

I grew up in France during the German occupation in World War II. My father served in the French Army, and after the Allies liberated France when I was eight, American soldiers came through our town. I left France in 1958 to start a new life in the United States, but I always cherished my French heritage and cuisine.

World War II shaped my childhood as Germany occupied France and my father joined the French Army. During the war, he became a prisoner of war, and I experienced the liberation of France by the Allies when I was eight. The war brought American soldiers into our lives and eventually influenced my decision to work for the US Army after the war.

My father was a proud member of the French Army during World War II. He left to serve when Germany occupied France, and unfortunately, he was captured and held as a prisoner of war. His service deeply affected our family during those difficult years.

My father spent time as a German prisoner of war during World War II after serving in the French Army. It was a very hard period for our family, filled with worry and uncertainty. His captivity was one of the many hardships we endured during the war.

The Allies liberated France when I was eight years old, bringing hope and freedom after years of occupation. American soldiers, part of the Allies, came through our town, and our family even hosted some of them. Their kindness and gifts like Hershey bars and peanut butter left a lasting impression on me.

General George S. Patton led the Third United States Army that passed through our town during the liberation of France. I remember the American GIs under his command were everywhere, and our family welcomed some into our home. It was my first encounter with Americans, and I liked their kindness and generosity.

The Third United States Army, commanded by General Patton, came through our town when France was liberated during World War II. American soldiers from this unit were everywhere, and our family hosted some of them. Their presence marked a turning point in the war and in my life.

After high school, I worked as a secretary for the United States Army starting in 1953 because I spoke English well. This job opened new opportunities for me and eventually led to my marriage to an American GI. The US Army played a big role in my transition from France to life in America.

In 1958, I moved to Virginia, Minnesota with my American husband, leaving France behind forever. It was a big change from my life in France, and I had to adjust to new foods and customs. Despite the differences, I found the people welcoming and made many friends there.

The United States became my new home after I married an American GI and moved here in 1958. I appreciated the warm welcome from people and adapted to American life, even though I missed French food and culture. Over time, I embraced both my French heritage and my American identity.

When my children were young, they wanted me to make Betty Crocker Angel Food cakes for their birthdays, which was quite different from the French desserts I usually baked. Over time, they preferred homemade French meringue tortes instead. Betty Crocker represented a part of American food culture that I encountered while raising my family here.
